transcript.text: Oh, good morning, everybody.
This meeting is being recorded.
Hey, Tyler, how's it going?
Going pretty good.
Sorry for the delay.
Was back-to-back this morning, and those are always tough.
I know we're a little bit late to get started, so maybe we could just go around the room with quick intros.
And I wasn't sure if you guys were, like, actively evaluating vendors for AI Search slash partners, so maybe just a bit more context on, you know, where you're at in the journey as well.
On my side, I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX.
I work hand-in-hand with our CEO, Marcel, and happy to loop him in if it makes sense during the evaluation.
I've met George before, so maybe we can start with you, David.
Hi, Tyler.
Good to meet you.
So I lead product marketing at Ataccama, the woman, but also I'm leading, let's say, the AI transformation.
I'm part of the team that's focusing on AI transformation within the company, which is...
really focused on how we use AI internally.
the fact that AI is a channel for us, a digital channel, that's a slightly different topic, but there's obviously a big relation to it as well.
So, yeah, I guess that would be my role, helping improve our efficiency within the marketing team.
Nice.
Good to meet you.
Likewise.
I can go next if you want.
I'm Sophie.
I'm VP of growth marketing.
So, my team spans across field, partner, and digital marketing.
So, my interest in this is obviously like everybody else in B2B marketing.
We are trying to compete for market share, mind share in AI search and discoverability.
We have been looking at different venues.
Sorry, I've just come off a call about events.
It's different vendors.
And we're also looking at kind of things that we can build in-house as well, just to be transparent.
Perfect.
I'm Maria.
I work under Sophie.
I'm the project manager for them.
I'm
But I'm going be helping a lot with all the AI search and running campaigns and everything.
Awesome.
Well, the high level on GrowthX is that we are an end-to-end partner.
So we're a little bit different than a typical agency where we're going to help you with everything from coming up with a strategy around AI search.
We typically are publishing daily for all of our customers.
So we do five articles per week.
Quality at scale is like a big part of what we do.
And then we also do all of the LLM visibility and monitoring.
We just launched our own platform.
We were partnered with Scrunch prior to this, which is a very popular vendor in the space, like Profound.
But now we actually have a platform that all of our clients get access to as part of the partnership.
Sorry, George, you have your hand up.
I think you were saying something before I jumped in.
Yeah.
Yeah, I was going to say just to level set around...
Like, you know, because this call is being pushed a little bit, so probably just to bring the Atacama people up to speed.
So growthx was referred to me by a former colleague of mine.
And basically, like what growthx specializes in is giving us visibility into how we're performing in LLM.
So like, you know, and basically helping us with AEO, but it tells us where and what to to enhance.
And like, Tyler, because like right now we have Scrunch.
Tyler, I know you mentioned that you guys initially had partnered with Scrunch.

But I want to kind of, you know, maybe if you don't mind telling the rest of the team, like kind of what you guys do that goes above and beyond just like something like as simple as like a Scrunch, for example.
Yeah, absolutely.
And I would say the core of what we're doing and why teams choose us is it is difficult to create content at the volume and quality required to drive meaningful visibility with AEO.

When you think about the long tail of how anyone can go in and have their own sort of unique conversation with the memory connected, we are really able to publish content, typically editorial, sometimes programmatic, and help a team that may have limited resources to do so internally, or doesn't want to spend the three to six months that it would take to get up to speed building workflows and tools like AirOps.

And so on the visibility and monitoring side, I'm still learning sort of how we differentiate there from partners like Scrunch and also like tools like Profound, but that's the core problem that we're solving, is really just helping teams publish content faster to drive visibility faster, more consistently, and leading that all the way through to increased traffic and increased conversions.
And I think, you know, the reason that...
that...
We're uniquely positioned is GrowthX.
We were founded by Marcel Santilli, who throughout his career, he's built organic content engines everywhere he's been.
So like take IBM, he built their first microsite called securityintelligence.com.
It was their highest ROI marketing channel for a number of years.
And then all the way through to Hatchi Corp through their hyper growth, service titan, scale AI as they were acquired by Meta.
What Marcel did at DeepGram was he actually built workflows at Aerobs.
And he experimented with where can I automate?
How can I accelerate the process?
There's nobody that's like really building massive teams of writers anymore, right?
So I clearly need to embrace using AI.
And they 24x their traffic in 18 months.
They 4x their revenue.
And Marcel started doing these workshops.
You'll see on LinkedIn, like he's a big build in public.
I.

And he was still CMO at DeepGram when he was doing these workshops, but that's what led him to start GrowthX, is just realizing that, you know, when you think about the options that are out there, AI tools are great, but can you scale quickly using, you know, those tools and trying to build workflows and refactor them as models change?
Agencies typically aren't doing things at the speed or the breadth that we're doing them.
And that's really kind of the core, you know, difference of GrowthX versus other organizations.

We have an internal platform that I'll show you where the workflows live, that our team of experts is using, but it really is this model of, you know, AI workflows, experts in the loop, being able to help brands like some of the logos that you see here, drive visibility without needing to spend all their time.
I'm building those workflows or focused on writing, you know, articles at the pace and the frequency that we're creating them.
Anyone have any questions?
High level.
I'd love to dig a little bit more into like the challenges that you're experiencing with organic growth.
And then I can maybe like tailor the pitch to, you know, the specifics of what you're facing right now.
Just a, maybe a dumb question.
So I can see, obviously you can do the tracking, you build the workflows, et cetera, et cetera.
Do you actually, like, what's your, what role do you play from a content creation perspective?
Obviously you're trying to automate the content creation as well, but is that just kind of shipped back to the client and then the client reviews or are you reviewing?
I'm just asking from, obviously, because you're an agency, like how, how much do you get involved versus how much are you building the infrastructure that the client then uses?
Maybe if I add a follow-up, because I have a similar question.
Like, I was surprised when you mentioned that, like, you have your own tool.
Like, maybe you will answer this question when you will show the product.
But, like, at first, I thought you were agency.
But then you mentioned you have a tool.
And, like, we would actually be using the tool.
So that, to me, sounds like SaaS business.
So if you can put some clarity into it, then maybe the demo is going to make it clear.
Yeah, absolutely.
So, Sophie, to your question, we are handling, like, the majority of the actual content creation and execution.
What the client is doing is they're helping us calibrate on the artifacts, which is the context that helps engineer our workflows to your business, your ICP, your brand voice and guidelines.
And then, you know, we're also having your team review the drafts of the articles that we create.
So that's really all we're asking from the clients is help us calibrate.
We're by week four and looking to publish around 10 by the end of the first phase.
And then when we move into the growth execution, that's when we're doing five articles per week.
And we're pretty flexible in terms of how we can execute.
So you can see like we've done editorial content is definitely like our bread and butter, but we've also done programmatic content.
We've done integrations pages.
We've done custom use cases like building out Lovable's templates library.
And in this example, we're actually building a custom pipeline that interacts with their API and then creates a real app that their visitors or users can go in and preview or remix.
And so, you know, at the end of the day, we're optimizing for all the text, but we do have forward deployed design engineers and AI engineers that can take the pipelines that we've built across.
Struggling when it comes to organic growth.
I'm trying to get myself off mute.
So we must be the only company in the history of B2B marketing that doesn't have a content team.
And so that presents us with a bit of a challenge.
We do have a content writer who's part time.
She's doing a huge amount of work in the time that she has.
But we are poor when it comes to having actual, like, dedicated content creators in the team.
Everybody wears that hat.
And so obviously when that happens, you can get a lot of inconsistency in the approach from a content perspective.
Because people have varying different degrees of experience.
And they're writing for very specific channels.
And so I think what we're trying to do is make sure that the content that we're putting out there not only is optimized for the new world of AI search, but it's also, it's a consistent.
And a standard across the different touch points and the different channels that we're using.
So I'd say that's the two ways that we've kind of come into this conversation.
One, the very early one, which was probably like six, seven months ago, maybe more than that now, was like everybody trying to understand how we're showing up in AI search.
How do we compare to a competition?
What are the opportunities for improvement?

And then like quickly that's moved into how do we create content at scale that can be optimized for those different models and combining that with our own resources and how we're trying to apply that over all of the work that we're trying to do.
So I think from my perspective, we've looked at a few vendors that were sort of initially in more of the analytics side of the house.
But very quickly, it feels like everyone's also started putting together content workflows and content operating systems.
So that world has converged kind of naturally on its own as well.
And obviously, as you said, you're hybriding that world between tech and experience across a large amount of clients that you can then multiply.
Makes sense.

So it sounds like the execution side is where there's the biggest need, because you haven't really been able to build content at scale, and you're sort of considering, should we do that through a partner, or should we do that through building our own workflows?
That's where you're at today.

Yeah, and it might end up being a mix, actually, to be honest, because I think there's some things that, I say we, I have no part in this, David has been able to build on his own, and there are some things that we're kind of working out.
Is it, you know, is it just more efficient, faster, etc., if we bring in some expertise or bring in some technology to support?

Yeah, like, it's obviously easier to build, but we only, we don't, we don't do it at scale, as you do, so we don't really have the best practices, and so on, so we just don't want to, like, jump to, let's build it, because it's easy.
Solution, because it might not be the best one.
So exploring both options.
Makes sense.
Yeah, let me help you see how the content gets created and what the future looks like, slash how quickly that future would get here.
Because I think from our perspective, we agree.
We don't want to have the burden of creating the content for every single customer we want to work with.
We want to empower the customers to be able to do that without our help or with our oversight.
And so to go through like a quick example and then break down the process, Augment Code, they compete against Cursor, GitHub.
It's in like the AI coding space.
They're a customer who we really put the foot on the gas with because they wanted to move fast.
We created over 130 guides for them.
A lot of these guides are pretty technical in nature just to give you a look at how we can fine tune the engine to the client that we're working with.
There's like code C.
C.
We've also created over 200, we call it the tools section.
Sometimes it's listicles, sometimes it's comparisons.
And then we also have this section, which is the learn section, that's more thought leadership.
And there's about, you know, 40 of these different learn resources.
And I've used that example, because with augment code, the search they care the most about is being the best AI coding tool for enterprises.
And we're positioning them above cursor.
And that answer, we're starting to see our articles get cited for other vendors.
And when you look at a search that's more focused on their differentiator, which is they can handle large code bases.
My computer will definitely be a little biased, but you start to see a lot more citations that are the articles that we've written.
And if you look at the traditional side, their organic traffic.
It has gone from about $20,000 monthly organic when they started.
They were also spending a lot on paid.
Now it's over 3x and they've almost shut off the paid ad spend.
So how do we get here, right?
What's our process that we follow?
In the strategy sprint, and I'll use engine as an example, we created all these business travel guides.
The first thing that we're going to do is we're going to understand the keyword universe against your top competitors so that we can actually go after opportunities that have search volume connected.
And from there, we're going to map everything into these different topic clusters where you want authority.
And then we'll create a huge roadmap of different content assignments and give our recommendations, right, around what we should prioritize.
But also this can be a collaboration, right?
If you have a certain level of confidence about a specific topic, then we can always start in different areas and let the data point us in the right directions.
And so what we do while we're creating this content OS is we'll create those artifacts that inform the workflows.
So for Engine, they wanted to be focused on different industries.
So we created artifacts across all of their target industries.
And you can see that when you think about AI search, we go super deep into what the construction operations manager is thinking relative to business travel.
Right.
This isn't just generic about the operations manager.
It's all their fears and uncertainties when it comes to business travel, motivations, how to win or lose their trust, pain points, why they choose Engine in particular.
And then the same thing for the finance manager and the project manager.
And so these will be like living artifacts that we update as we learn more from you throughout the partnership.
You can see that for the writing guidelines, you know, there's been like 25 iterations.
for this specific artifact.
And this is like good examples, bad examples.
How should we structure the content?
How should we, which language and terminology should we use or avoid?
And so by doing all this work up front, and even creating custom artifacts over time, for example, weaving in case studies when it makes sense, that's how we solve for being able to scale.
We want to get the context right first.
Any questions about the first part of our process?
Because this is a pretty important piece.
I have a question about the volume.
So not really this part.
This is clear what comes to the prep, but when it comes to the execution, so like there's going to be like hundreds of keywords or prompts that we want to optimize for.
So that is going to be like at least higher tens, maybe like lower hundreds of assets eventually, like what's the scale you are actually able to provide?
I didn't like the costs you, for example, had in the deck, which was like 40k for the setup, 20k for the execution, if I remember correctly.
Yeah, we can definitely go faster than five articles per week, if that's what you'd prefer.
And if we find a good programmatic use case, obviously quantity is, you know, less of a factor.
It's more about getting the inputs right.
This is a good example of what we did for Ramp.
We created this per diem calculator.
And so this was one where we had our design engineers involved to, you know, create the interactive map and the configuration around the rate calculator.

But the idea here is that if someone searches any city in America, along with, you know, what's the average per diem or per diem Houston or New York, whatever, there's a page, right, that we've created for Ramp that helps them optimize for that search across all the different variations.
So the short answer is we can absolutely increase quantity if that's what.
work.
the short answer So Thank

We keep it pretty flexible.

But if you were looking for us to execute on editorial and programmatic and we could switch gears between the two, then we don't have to increase the cost for moving from the five editorial a week to something like 25 or more programmatic a week.
So I think when we kind of hone in on what the actual scope is here, that's when we can make things more clear in terms of the cost.

It's drafting an article for nine minutes.
And the inputs are those artifacts, right?
So we've got inputs around what this specific topic we're writing about is, the writing guidelines for this piece, the personas that we're specifically targeting.
And then in the background, what's happening is, we're generating a research plan with an objective, with all these questions to answer, success criteria, source requirements.
And then we're synthesizing the results across all these.
Is a different workflow in our catalog.
And this is how we're able to customize things for our clients.
We take the workflows that we use across all clients and then we find ways to optimize either through the inputs, the artifacts, or through creating a custom step in the process.

But if you looked at a more unique use case, so we've created a lot of content for Biologica.

All of these different articles that we've done here.
And so for the Biologica example, if I actually go into...

And so, you know, this is kind of just like a quick look for our first call today of like what's happening in the background, but it includes cross-linking across your site, finding the most relevant source links.
You'll notice that as we draft the article, the writing guidelines are used as an input, but we'll also have that as a separate step in the process after the article's been written.

We do the research up front, but for fact-checking, we also take the entire article, we split it into different chunks, and then we do more deep research to actually validate whether what we wrote is accurate.
